message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 14
Recent changes: none

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll execute the Refactor phase. Let me start by loading the architecture memory and reading the current state of the code.

src

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` → `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
26	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver — the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 → ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body — both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100×1.5×1.3×1.1 = 214.5 → round→215, ceil→215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 × 0.80 × 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil → 53, +5 = 58 — also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the round→ceil switch until a test actually fails.
27	- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
31	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8–9 (loyalty discount: `100 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 × 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil → 28) — every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
32	- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`; replaced magic `>= 2` in the loyalty ternary with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline — every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. The spec's domain phrase "loyal customer ≥2 years" now reads in the code. Mass impact negligible (+1 binding, -1 constant from inline literal moving to named binding). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (cycle-8 reasoning unchanged — the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature, so extracting now locks in the wrong shape). Cycle 10 (rune 33) is a pure `BASE_PRICE` table addition — zero edits to `runScenario` predicted.
40	- Naming established: numeric thresholds that gate a multiplier follow the `*_THRESHOLD` suffix (sibling to `*_MULTIPLIER`); both stand together to read as "if customer crosses THRESHOLD, apply MULTIPLIER".
41	
42	## After cycle 10
43	- Change: reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries from insertion order (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25) to descending-by-value (sword 100, staff 80, amulet 60, potion 40, rune 25). `runScenario` body untouched. Cycle-10 delta itself (`rune: 25`) was absorbed by the cycle-2 lookup table with zero edits to the pricing pipeline, as predicted in cycle 9.
44	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), presentation tier. With five entries now, insertion order no longer carries meaning (sword and amulet were just the first two test cases); descending-by-value lets a reader scan the price ladder at a glance and immediately see which items are cheap (rune) vs premium (sword). Zero behavioural risk — `Record<string, number>` key order doesn't affect lookup semantics. Mass unchanged (~42). Resisted (again) extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: cycles 11–13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price — `priceForItem` with an `Item` signature locks in the wrong shape because building blocks operate on item *groups*, not single items. Cycle 14 (two-quote scenario with post-first-contract discount 0.85) introduces a quote-index dependency that will force the customer-discount extraction with the right signature. Defer both extractions until those tests provide the driver.
45	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` table is sorted descending by value; new entries (moonstone in cycle 13) should be inserted at the correct position to preserve the price ladder.
46	
47	## After cycle 11
48	- Change: extracted module constant `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE = 60` with a comment explaining that three alike "building-block" pieces (rune, moonstone, …) pool to a single unit priced at this fixed tier, independent of the per-piece `BASE_PRICE`. Replaced inline `buildingBlocks * 60` with `buildingBlocks * BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE`. Otherwise `runScenario` body structure (rune-count → buildingBlocks/looseRunes → loop excluding rune → add pooled+loose contribution) untouched.
49	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The bare `60` was the only remaining unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline AND it was actively misleading — a reader could plausibly read it as `BASE_PRICE.amulet` (which is also 60), conflating two semantically distinct rules. The comment + named constant disambiguates: building-block tier ≠ amulet base. This matters because cycle 13 (three moonstones also pool to 60) will confirm the rule generalises beyond runes — moonstone base will be different, but its 3-pool price will also be 60, so the constant carries its semantics correctly across types. Mass impact ~0 (binding+invocation in vs. literal+(inline) out is a wash). Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)`: cycle 12 (four runes = 1 block + 1 loose) is absorbed by the existing block/loose split with zero changes; cycle 13 (rune AND moonstone, two pooled types) is the real driver — that's when the hardcoded `i.type === "rune"` check on lines 38 and 43 becomes a duplicated string-literal type test against a hypothetical `POOLED_TYPES` set, and the extraction signature can be designed against two concrete examples instead of one. Extracting now with only rune in mind would lock the API around a single-pooled-type assumption.
50	- Naming established: `BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE` is the pool-tier price for any three-alike grouping (rune today, moonstone in cycle 13); it is intentionally separate from `BASE_PRICE` (which is per-piece) even when they share a numeric value. The "pooled types" set is currently inlined as the literal string `"rune"` in two places — cycle 13 will need to convert these to a shared `POOLED_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])` or equivalent.
51	
52	## After cycle 12
53	- Change: extracted `quoteItemsSubtotal(items: Item[]): number` containing the entire per-quote item-pricing block (rune-count → buildingBlocks/looseRunes → loop excluding rune → add pooled+loose contribution). `runScenario` body collapses to four readable lines: destructure → `quoteItemsSubtotal(steps[0].items)` → `loyaltyMultiplier` ternary → premium formula (`ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemsTotal × loyalty × first-insurance) + ADMIN_FEE`).
54	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) finally has a driver — the cycle-12 four-runes test was absorbed with zero edits (as predicted in cycle 11), but the resulting body had reached 17 lines mixing two abstraction levels: (a) "compute per-quote item subtotal with pooling" and (b) "wrap subtotal in customer-loyalty + first-insurance + admin-fee envelope". The pipeline now reads as a four-step formula at the top level. Crucially, the helper signature `(items: Item[]) → number` is the right shape for BOTH upcoming forces: cycle 13 (moonstone joins inside the helper, no `runScenario`-level change), and cycle 14 (sword+amulet in one quote — already absorbed: helper iterates all items), and cycle 15 (two-quote scenario — `runScenario` will map over `steps`, calling `quoteItemsSubtotal` per quote). Module mass net +3 (one new invocation, one parameter binding); `runScenario` body mass dropped from ~89 to ~30; helper carries ~60 with a single concern. Rule 2 > APP per project conventions, but here APP barely moves anyway. Still resisting `POOLED_TYPES` set / `priceForItemGroup` extraction — cycle 13's moonstone is the only correct driver for that, and lifting it now still locks the wrong shape (with only rune as exemplar the signature can't see the two-type generalisation).
55	- Naming established: `quoteItemsSubtotal(items)` is the boundary between item-level pricing (BASE_PRICE × itemModifierMultiplier, plus pooling) and customer/contract-level pricing (loyalty × first-insurance + admin fee). New per-quote pricing rules (moonstone pooling, sword+amulet multi-item, future bundle discounts) go INSIDE this helper. New per-customer/per-contract rules (post-first-contract discount in cycle 15) go in `runScenario` AROUND the helper call.
56	
57	## After cycle 13
58	- Change: merged the two passes over `items` in `quoteItemsSubtotal` into a single if/else partition — the pooled branch accumulates into `pooledCounts`, the non-pooled branch prices inline into `subtotal`. The follow-up loop over `pooledCounts` (block + loose finalisation) is unchanged. Module structure otherwise untouched (BASE_PRICE table gained `moonstone: 25`, POOLED_TYPES set added in Green; both correct as Green left them).
59	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) had a clear driver — the second loop existed only to repeat `POOLED_TYPES.has(item.type)` and dispatch the non-pooled half of the same partition Green had already started in the first loop. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): one loop, one conditional check, and one invocation removed (APP mass ~70 → ~60 inside the helper). Rule 2 preserved: the if/else reads cleanly as "for each item, either pool it or price it inline", and the finalisation loop's role ("turn pooled counts into block + loose contributions") is now visually distinct rather than buried as a third loop after two near-duplicates. The structure also pre-positions cycle 14 (sword+amulet) and cycle 15 (two-quote scenario) just as well as the two-loop version did — both go through the unified partition without further edits. Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)` — the pooling finalisation is now only 3 lines and a single concern, and the right driver for that extraction is a future test where pooled-group pricing itself gets a modifier (e.g. cursed runes, or a different block size); none of cycles 14–24 introduces that.
60	- Naming established: the if/else inside `quoteItemsSubtotal` is the canonical "partition + price" shape for per-item pricing; new pooled types just join `POOLED_TYPES`, new per-piece pricing variations attach via `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` inside the non-pooled branch.
61

content.text: Now let me run the tests to confirm they are all green, then evaluate refactoring options.

> vitest run


 RUN  v1.6.1 /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking

 ✓ src/claim-office.spec.ts  (24 tests | 10 skipped) 4ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed | 10 todo (24)
   Start at  20:31:16
   Duration  188ms (transform 34ms, setup 0ms, collect 48ms, tests 4ms, environment 0ms, prepare 47ms)

content.text:
All 14 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Current public surface and key bindings:
- `runScenario(scenario)`: matches contract, clear
- `quoteItemsSubtotal(items)`: accurate — sum of per-quote items (with pooling)
- `itemModifierMultiplier(item)`: accurate
- `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)`: domain phrase from spec
- Module constants: all spelled out
- Local `loyaltyMultiplier`, `itemsTotal`, `premium`: clear
- Inside `quoteItemsSubtotal`: `subtotal`, `pooledCounts`, `buildingBlocks`, `loose`, `count`, `type` — clear

**Decision: Keep current names.** Cycle-13 added the sword+amulet test (cycle 14 per spec listing) and the prior architecture decisions stand — all names reveal current intent.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

Approximate mass of `claim-office.ts` (focus on `runScenario` + `quoteItemsSubtotal`, ignoring type aliases and module constants which are structural):

**`runScenario` (body)**:
- Bindings: customer, steps, itemsTotal, loyaltyMultiplier, premium, scenario = 6 = 6
- Invocations: `quoteItemsSubtotal`, `ceilInMhpcoFavor`, `*` (×2), `+` (×1) = 5 = 10
- Conditionals: 1 ternary = 4
- Constants: LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD ref counted as binding; literal 0 absent in body; `1` in ternary = 1
- Total: ~21

**`quoteItemsSubtotal` (body)**:
- Bindings: items, subtotal, pooledCounts, item, type, count, buildingBlocks, loose = 8 = 8
- Constants: 0, 3, 0, 3, 3 (in literals) ≈ 5 = 5
- Invocations: POOLED_TYPES.has, set, get, ?? , BASE_PRICE[], itemModifierMultiplier, Math.floor, *, %, *, + (rough), entries iter ≈ ~12 = ~24
- Conditionals: 1 if/else = 4
- Loops: 2 for-of = 10
- Assignments: subtotal += (×2), pooledCounts.set = ~3 = ~18
- Total: ~69

**Total Module Mass: ~90**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass — yes
### Rule 2: Reveals Intent

One thing catches my eye: in `quoteItemsSubtotal`, the finalization line:
```
subtotal += buildingBlocks * BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE + loose * BASE_PRICE[type];
```

This mixes two priced contributions (pooled block contribution + loose remainder) into one arithmetic expression where `+` precedence matters. Splitting into two named contributions could improve scannability. But this is at the boundary of clarity vs. element-count — currently it reads fine for someone who knows precedence.

Another observation: the helper `itemModifierMultiplier` is only invoked from the non-pooled branch. This is correct (cycle 6 established this scope), but it means pooled items don't get item-attribute multipliers. The spec hasn't asked for "cursed runes" so this is correct silence, not a bug.

### Rule 3: No Duplication
Looking carefully: `BASE_PRICE[type]` appears in both the non-pooled branch and the pooled finalisation (for loose pieces). That's not real duplication — they price different conceptual things (whole-piece pricing vs. leftover-piece pricing). Both legitimately need the per-piece base. No extraction needed.

The `(... ?? 0) + 1` map increment pattern appears once — no duplication.

### Rule 4: Fewest Elements
The structure is already lean. No unused bindings.

## Candidate Refactoring

One small Rule-2 improvement is available: extract the pooled finalisation contribution into a named local to make the addition read as "block contribution plus loose contribution":

```typescript
for (const [type, count] of pooledCounts) {
  const buildingBlocks = Math.floor(count / 3);
  const loose = count % 3;
  const blockContribution = buildingBlocks * BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE;
  const looseContribution = loose * BASE_PRICE[type];
  subtotal += blockContribution + looseContribution;
}
```

This adds 2 bindings (+2 mass) and 1 assignment expression already there. Trade-off: adds ~4 mass but names the two semantically distinct pieces. However, the current line is already readable and idiomatic; extracting two single-use variables feels speculative (Rule 4 cautions against this).

Another candidate: the per-quote premium formula in `runScenario` runs three multiplications. Currently:
```
ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemsTotal * loyaltyMultiplier * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE
```

This is already a clear linear pipeline. No improvement available.

## A Real Refactoring Available

Looking at `runScenario` more carefully — the line:
```typescript
customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1
```

…is anonymous "what kind of multiplier is this?" — when read at the `runScenario` level it's a customer-side multiplier inline as a ternary. Cycle-8 explicitly resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` because "the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature."

The very next test in the list (cycle 15, currently `it.todo`) is exactly that two-quote test: it introduces a post-first-contract discount (0.85) that depends on quote-index. So extracting now with a `customer`-only signature would lock in the wrong shape — cycle 15 will need to pass either the contract-history state or the quote index, which makes the signature different.

**Decision: Defer customer-discount extraction.** The architecture-notes lineage (cycles 8, 10, 12) has been consistent and accurate — the next test will provide the proper driver.

## Naming touch-up

One more pass on the binding `itemsTotal` in `runScenario`. The helper returns the "subtotal" (item-side only, pre-loyalty, pre-loading). The local is named `itemsTotal` — slightly inconsistent with the helper name `quoteItemsSubtotal`. Renaming `itemsTotal` → `itemsSubtotal` would align it with the helper's vocabulary and make clear it is the pre-multiplier figure, not the final total.

This is a pure naming change, Rule 2, ~0 mass change, zero behavioural risk.

content.text: Tests still pass. Let me update task status to mark cycle 14 complete.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The rename `itemsTotal` → `itemsSubtotal` is purely cosmetic — same number of bindings, same arithmetic, same invocations. **Mass unchanged: ~90 → ~90.**

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): Renamed local binding `itemsTotal` → `itemsSubtotal` to mirror the helper name `quoteItemsSubtotal` and signal that this value is the pre-loyalty / pre-loading / pre-admin figure (a subtotal), not the final total.
- Mass change: ~90 → ~90 (binding-name length is free in APP).

**Refactorings Deliberately Deferred** (per architecture-notes lineage):
- `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction — cycle 15 (`it.todo` next) introduces post-first-contract discount tied to quote-index; a customer-only signature would lock in the wrong shape. Cycles 8, 10, 12 all forecast this exact deferral.
- `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)` — the pooling block is 3 lines and a single concern; no test in the queue introduces a modifier on a pooled group.
- Splitting the `subtotal += buildingBlocks * BUILDING_BLOCK_PRICE + loose * BASE_PRICE[type]` line into two named intermediates — Rule 4 wins; current line is idiomatic with standard precedence, naming two single-use intermediates would be speculative.

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 13
- Change: merged the two passes over `items` in `quoteItemsSubtotal` into a single if/else partition — the pooled branch accumulates into `pooledCounts`, the non-pooled branch prices inline into `subtotal`. The follow-up loop over `pooledCounts` (block + loose finalisation) is unchanged. Module structure otherwise untouched (BASE_PRICE table gained `moonstone: 25`, POOLED_TYPES set added in Green; both correct as Green left them).
- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) had a clear driver — the second loop existed only to repeat `POOLED_TYPES.has(item.type)` and dispatch the non-pooled half of the same partition Green had already started in the first loop. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): one loop, one conditional check, and one invocation removed (APP mass ~70 → ~60 inside the helper). Rule 2 preserved: the if/else reads cleanly as "for each item, either pool it or price it inline", and the finalisation loop's role ("turn pooled counts into block + loose contributions") is now visually distinct rather than buried as a third loop after two near-duplicates. The structure also pre-positions cycle 14 (sword+amulet) and cycle 15 (two-quote scenario) just as well as the two-loop version did — both go through the unified partition without further edits. Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)` — the pooling finalisation is now only 3 lines and a single concern, and the right driver for that extraction is a future test where pooled-group pricing itself gets a modifier (e.g. cursed runes, or a different block size); none of cycles 14–24 introduces that.
- Naming established: the if/else inside `quoteItemsSubtotal` is the canonical "partition + price" shape for per-item pricing; new pooled types just join `POOLED_TYPES`, new per-piece pricing variations attach via `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` inside the non-pooled branch.
input.new_string:
## After cycle 13
- Change: merged the two passes over `items` in `quoteItemsSubtotal` into a single if/else partition — the pooled branch accumulates into `pooledCounts`, the non-pooled branch prices inline into `subtotal`. The follow-up loop over `pooledCounts` (block + loose finalisation) is unchanged. Module structure otherwise untouched (BASE_PRICE table gained `moonstone: 25`, POOLED_TYPES set added in Green; both correct as Green left them).
- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) had a clear driver — the second loop existed only to repeat `POOLED_TYPES.has(item.type)` and dispatch the non-pooled half of the same partition Green had already started in the first loop. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): one loop, one conditional check, and one invocation removed (APP mass ~70 → ~60 inside the helper). Rule 2 preserved: the if/else reads cleanly as "for each item, either pool it or price it inline", and the finalisation loop's role ("turn pooled counts into block + loose contributions") is now visually distinct rather than buried as a third loop after two near-duplicates. The structure also pre-positions cycle 14 (sword+amulet) and cycle 15 (two-quote scenario) just as well as the two-loop version did — both go through the unified partition without further edits. Resisted (still) extracting `priceForItemGroup(type, count)` / `bundleItems(items)` — the pooling finalisation is now only 3 lines and a single concern, and the right driver for that extraction is a future test where pooled-group pricing itself gets a modifier (e.g. cursed runes, or a different block size); none of cycles 14–24 introduces that.
- Naming established: the if/else inside `quoteItemsSubtotal` is the canonical "partition + price" shape for per-item pricing; new pooled types just join `POOLED_TYPES`, new per-piece pricing variations attach via `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` inside the non-pooled branch.

## After cycle 14
- Change: renamed local binding `itemsTotal` → `itemsSubtotal` in `runScenario` so it mirrors the helper name `quoteItemsSubtotal` it receives its value from. Cycle-14 delta itself (sword + amulet in one quote) was absorbed by the cycle-12/13 partition in `quoteItemsSubtotal` with zero edits to `runScenario` body, as predicted in cycle 12.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), micro-tier. The binding represents the pre-loyalty, pre-first-insurance, pre-admin-fee figure — a subtotal, not a total. Calling it `Total` next to a `Subtotal`-named helper invited a reader to assume the helper was doing more than it does (or that the local was already the final premium). The two now share vocabulary: `itemsSubtotal = quoteItemsSubtotal(items)`. Mass impact zero (binding-name length is free in APP). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction: cycle 15 (the next test, two-quote scenario with second-quote `× 0.85` post-first-contract discount) provides the proper driver — that test introduces both a second customer-side multiplier AND a quote-index dependency, so the extraction signature can be designed against the right forces. Cycle 15's two-quote shape will also require `runScenario` to map over `steps` instead of indexing `steps[0]`; that's a `runScenario`-body change, not a helper change.
- Naming established: local bindings that receive a helper's return value should mirror the helper's named role (`subtotal` here), not abbreviate or reinterpret it.

**Refactor Phase Complete:**

- **Refactoring**: Renamed local `itemsTotal` → `itemsSubtotal` in `runScenario` to mirror helper name `quoteItemsSubtotal` and signal it is the pre-loyalty/pre-loading/pre-admin figure (Rule 2: Reveal Intent, micro-tier).
- **Mass Change**: ~90 → ~90 (binding-name length is free in APP)
- **Tests**: All 14 passing.

**Deferred (with rationale)**:
- `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction — cycle 15 (next `it.todo`) introduces post-first-contract discount with quote-index dependency, which would force a different signature than customer-only.
- `priceForItemGroup` / `bundleItems` — no driver in queue (no modifier on pooled groups in cycles 15-24).
- Splitting the `blockContribution + looseContribution` line into named intermediates — Rule 4 wins; current line is idiomatic, naming two single-use intermediates would be speculative.
